title: The connection between seasonal changes and trauma activation
author: Kalesh
excerpt: Here's the thing. October hits and something shifts inside you. Not just the temperature drop or the shorter days. Something deeper. Something in your body tha...
metaDescription: Here's the thing. October hits and something shifts inside you. Not just the temperature drop or the shorter days. Something deeper. Something in your bod...
htmlContent:
Here's the thing. October hits and something shifts inside you. Not just the temperature drop or the shorter days. Something deeper. Something in your body that you can't quite name but you can definitely feel.

Maybe your sleep gets worse. Maybe your irritability spikes for no reason you can identify. Maybe you find yourself crying at things that wouldn't normally touch you. Or maybe you just feel heavy. A bone-deep tiredness that sleep doesn't fix.

If this sounds familiar, you're not broken. You're not making it up. And you're definitely not alone.

What you're experiencing might be what trauma researchers call seasonal activation. The way your nervous system responds to environmental changes, especially the shift from one season to another, can trigger old survival responses that your body learned long ago.

Let me explain what I mean.

What seasonal changes actually do to a traumatized nervous system

Your nervous system is always scanning. Always. That's its job. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, calls this process "neuroception." It's your body's automatic detection system for safety and danger. And here's the thing about neuroception, it doesn't just scan for obvious threats like a car speeding toward you or someone yelling at you.

It scans for patterns. Rhythms. Environmental cues that might signal something is off.

When the seasons change, the sensory information your nervous system receives changes dramatically. Less light. Colder air. Different sounds. The smell of rain or frost instead of summer heat. Your body notices all of this, even if your conscious mind doesn't.

For someone with a history of trauma, these sensory shifts can feel threatening. Not because the change itself is dangerous. But because your nervous system learned, somewhere along the way, that change equals danger. That shifts in your environment might mean something bad is coming.

Think about it. If you grew up in a home where the atmosphere could shift without warning, where a parent's mood could turn on a dime, your body learned to associate environmental changes with impending threat. The drop in temperature, the dimming light, the different sounds outside, your nervous system flags them all as potential warnings.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body's sensations. Not in the story you tell about what happened. In the actual physical sensations that your body holds onto. When the seasons change, those sensations can get stirred up. Your body might start producing the same tension patterns, the same shallow breathing, the same hypervigilance that it learned during the original traumatic events.

You might not even connect it to the season. You might just think you're having a bad month. Or that your anxiety is acting up again. Or that you're just tired for no reason.

But your body knows. It always knows.

Why fall and spring hit hardest for trauma survivors

I've noticed something in my own recovery and in talking with other trauma survivors. Fall and spring tend to be the roughest transitions. Not winter or summer themselves, but the shifts into and out of them.

There's a reason for this.

Fall brings decreasing light, which affects your circadian rhythms. Your melatonin production changes. Your cortisol patterns shift. For a nervous system that's already dysregulated, these biological changes can feel destabilizing. Your body is literally operating on a different chemical schedule than it was six weeks ago.

Spring brings the opposite. More light. More activity. More social expectations. For someone whose nervous system learned to survive by staying small and hidden, the world suddenly getting brighter and louder can feel overwhelming.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who works extensively with structural dissociation, talks about how different parts of the self can get activated by different environmental cues. You might have a part that remembers being abandoned in winter. Or a part that associates summer with being trapped at home with no escape. When the season triggers that part, you don't just remember the feeling. You relive it. Your body goes right back into that old survival state.

This is why you might find yourself reacting to the changing seasons in ways that don't make logical sense. Your conscious mind knows that autumn is just autumn. But some part of you remembers that autumn meant something else entirely.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium plays a role in regulating the nervous system and can help with sleep and muscle tension during these transitional periods. It's not a cure, but it can take the edge off.

What happens when your body remembers trauma through the senses

Here's something that doesn't get talked about enough. Trauma is stored in the sensory system. Not just in the memories you can narrate. In the actual sensations of your body.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," showed through decades of research that trauma changes how your body processes sensory information. The smell of a particular cologne. The quality of light at a certain time of day. The feeling of a certain fabric against your skin. Any of these can trigger a trauma response, even if you don't consciously remember why.

Seasons come with a whole constellation of sensory information. The way the air smells when it's about to snow. The specific quality of light in late October. The sound of leaves blowing across pavement. The feeling of cold air on your face after months of warmth.

If your trauma happened during a particular season, your body might start preparing for danger weeks before that season actually arrives. It's like your nervous system has its own internal calendar. It knows what's coming, even if your conscious mind hasn't made the connection.

I remember a client who would get severely anxious every September, starting around Labor Day. She couldn't figure out why. She didn't have any specific memories tied to that time of year. But as we worked together, she realized that September was when school started. And school was where she had been bullied relentlessly for three years. Her body remembered the dread of September even though her mind had filed that chapter away as "over and done with."

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk's work explains exactly how the body holds onto trauma and why sensory triggers, including seasonal ones, can feel so overwhelming.

Notice what just happened in your body when I mentioned that. Did your shoulders tighten? Did your breath change? Did you feel a familiar heaviness somewhere in your chest or stomach? That's your body talking to you. That's the information that matters.

What actually helps when seasonal activation hits

Okay, so you're in the middle of a seasonal shift and your nervous system is freaking out. What do you actually do about it? Not the platitudes. Not the "just breathe through it" advice that makes you want to throw something. What actually helps.

First, name it. Say to yourself, "My nervous system is responding to seasonal change. This is a biological response, not a character flaw." That alone can drop your activation level by a few degrees. Your brain needs to know that what you're experiencing makes sense.

Second, give your body extra sensory input that signals safety. Weighted blankets. Warm drinks. Soft lighting. Repetitive sounds. Anything that tells your nervous system, "We are safe here. The environment is predictable." Your body needs to feel safe before it can calm down. No amount of positive thinking will override a nervous system that's still scanning for danger.

Third, adjust your expectations. If you know that October through December tends to be rough for you, plan accordingly. Don't schedule a million social obligations. Don't start new projects that require high performance. Give yourself permission to do less. To rest more. To be a little slower and a little softer with yourself.

Fourth, track your patterns across multiple years. Keep a simple log of when your symptoms spike. After a few seasons, you might start to see clear patterns. And when you can predict when the hard times will come, you can prepare for them instead of being blindsided every time.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Janina Fisher's approach can help you understand why different parts of you get triggered by different environmental cues, including seasonal changes.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). If your trauma involved caregivers who were unpredictable, this book can help you understand why your nervous system learned to be so sensitive to environmental shifts.

The uncomfortable truth about seasonal activation

Here's the part I can't soften for you. Some seasons are going to be hard for the rest of your life. Not because you're doing recovery wrong. Not because you haven't healed enough. But because your nervous system learned something real, and that learning doesn't just disappear.

Recovery isn't about becoming someone who never gets triggered. It's about becoming someone who can recognize when they're triggered and respond with compassion instead of self-attack.

So if you're reading this in the middle of a seasonal shift that's kicking your ass, I see you. I know how disorienting it is to feel like you're regressing when you thought you were doing better. I know how tempting it is to tell yourself that you should be past this by now.

But here's what I want you to hold onto. Your body's response to seasonal change isn't a sign that you're broken. It's a sign that your body remembers how to survive. And that same body, the one that learned to protect you through all those hard seasons, can also learn to find safety in the changing world.

It just takes time. And patience. And a whole lot of compassion for the parts of you that still brace themselves when the air gets cold and the light starts to fade.

Stay with me here. You're not alone in this.
